content.text: Why Stakeholder Mapping Is a Delivery Prerequisite

A Claude integration touches more stakeholders than the team that builds it. The BU director who owns the budget, the CISO who must approve the security model, the data owners who control the knowledge base, the front-line users who will or will not adopt the tool, and the legal team who must sign the DPA all have different interests in the programme's outcome. Managing these interests is not optional ceremony — it determines whether the integration goes live and whether it stays in use.

Stakeholder mapping is the structured process of identifying every individual and group whose behaviour can affect the Claude programme, understanding their current position (supportive, neutral, resistant), and selecting the right engagement strategy for each. A stakeholder map completed in week one of discovery prevents the most common enterprise AI project failure: a technically complete integration that cannot get approved, funded, or adopted because the wrong people were surprised at the wrong time.

The foundational tool for stakeholder mapping is the power-interest grid. It places each stakeholder on two dimensions: organisational power (ability to make decisions, control resources, or block progress) and interest (stake in the programme's outcome, whether positive or negative).

attrs.alt: Stakeholder Power-Interest Grid showing four quadrants: Manage Closely, Keep Satisfied, Keep Informed, and Monitor

content.text: The four quadrants generate four distinct engagement strategies. The strategies are not fixed — as the programme advances, stakeholders move between quadrants. A CISO who was 'Keep Satisfied' becomes 'Manage Closely' when a security review uncovers a data residency concern that requires their active involvement. The grid must be updated every 4–6 weeks throughout the programme lifecycle.
The grid cannot be populated without first identifying the complete stakeholder set. Common omissions that cause problems later:

Teams that use data outputs from systems that will be changed or replaced by the Claude integration. If Claude replaces a manual report that feeds another team's dashboard, that team is a stakeholder.

Internal audit, regulatory reporting, and risk functions often have veto power over data handling changes. They are commonly omitted from early engagement because they are not directly involved in delivery.

In unionised environments, AI tools that change job scope or replace tasks may require works council consultation under collective agreements. Discovering this late can add 6–12 weeks to a deployment timeline.

For customer-facing or partner-facing integrations, external stakeholders (key customers, channel partners, regulators) may have a legitimate interest that must be managed — even if they have no internal organisational authority.

Run a 60-minute stakeholder identification workshop at the start of discovery, with the BU sponsor, the programme delivery manager, and 2–3 team members who understand the current process being automated. The format:
Start with the directly affected team: who does this task today, and who reviews or approves their work?
Expand to the data layer: who owns the data sources, who receives the outputs, who audits the records?
Expand to approval chains: who must approve a change to this process (legal, compliance, IT security, finance)?
Expand to adjacent processes: which upstream and downstream processes will change when this one changes?
Check for external stakeholders: do customers, partners, or regulators interact with this process?

Output is a raw stakeholder list. After the workshop, the programme delivery manager scores each stakeholder on power and interest and places them on the grid. The map is reviewed with the BU sponsor before finalisation.
The grid tells you the engagement strategy, but it does not tell you what to say. A stakeholder profile adds the qualitative context needed to craft effective communications and manage resistance:
Personalise communications; confirm correct decision authority
What they care about in the programme's outcome (positive or negative)
Lead with their interest in every communication; address their concerns first
Detailed reports vs. exec summaries; email vs. verbal; formal vs. informal
Match format and channel to preference; a CFO who prefers one-page summaries will not read a 20-page technical report
Track sentiment changes; early resistance that is not addressed becomes late blocking
Who do they trust; who influences them
When direct persuasion fails, identify who can carry the message more effectively
Specific objections or risks they have raised
Prepare evidence-based responses to each named concern before the next engagement
What has been communicated, when, and their response
Maintain continuity; avoid re-explaining things they already know

A 20–30 minute conversation with each key stakeholder at the start of discovery. Ask about their current experience with the process being automated, their concerns about AI tools, and their definition of a successful outcome.

The BU sponsor knows the internal landscape. A 30-minute conversation before formal stakeholder engagement reveals informal dynamics, historical conflicts, and preferred communication styles that no formal directory would capture.
Past AI or digital transformation projects in the organisation reveal resistance patterns. If a previous automation project failed due to union concerns, that risk is likely present again.
Previous presentations to the CISO or legal team reveal what framings worked and what caused concern. Reuse what worked.

attrs.alt: Influence Strategy Framework showing Champion, Gatekeeper, Observer, and Monitor strategies

content.text: Each quadrant of the power-interest grid requires a different influence approach. Applying the wrong strategy wastes time and can create adversarial dynamics. Treating a Gatekeeper like a Champion (over-investing in enthusiastic engagement with a CISO who just wants compliance assurance) is as counterproductive as treating a Champion like a Monitor (under-informing a BU director who is your programme's executive sponsor).

Champions have both the power to advance the programme and the motivation to do so. The risk with champions is misusing their enthusiasm — over-relying on a single champion who then moves to a different role, or allowing the champion to over-promise outcomes the programme cannot deliver.
Establish regular bilateral catch-ups (every 2 weeks during active delivery) — not just formal gate reviews
Equip them to advocate: provide crisp talking points, one-page summaries, and early access to results they can share
Set realistic expectations: champions lose credibility if they over-promise on your behalf; brief them on risks and timelines before they engage upward
Identify and develop a secondary champion: if the primary champion leaves or deprioritises the programme, a secondary champion in a different part of the organisation maintains momentum

Gatekeepers — high power, low interest — are most commonly the CISO, the CFO, and the legal or compliance function. They do not need to become enthusiastic about Claude; they need to not block it. The goal is to address their concerns before they become blocking issues, and to secure their approval without demanding their ongoing attention.
A CISO's job is to prevent security incidents. Open with 'Here's how we're managing the three security risks you told us mattered most' — not with 'Here's what the system does.'
'We've tested this and it's fine' creates more concern than confidence. Provide the vendor DPA, the data flow diagram, the penetration test results, and the monitoring controls.

A Gatekeeper who encounters new information in a formal gate review meeting is more likely to defer or reject than approve. Informal pre-briefing removes surprises and gives them time to process concerns privately before the formal decision.

CFOs and CISOs who hear 'this will replace X people' or 'this will access all our data' go into risk mode. Frame as 'AI-assisted workflow with human approval step' until formal reassurance channels are established.

The most common gatekeeper failure mode is the programme team interpreting 'no objection' as endorsement. A CISO who has not said no has not said yes. Explicitly request sign-off on the specific items that require their approval — do not assume silence means approval.

Observers — low power, high interest — are typically the front-line users, team leads, and data owners who will live with the Claude integration daily. They cannot block the programme, but they can undermine adoption. A pilot that front-line users treat as imposed rather than co-created will produce low engagement data that kills the business case for scaling.
Involve representative users in the use case discovery workshop — their friction points are the real requirements
Run a structured pilot feedback process, not just a satisfaction survey: weekly 20-minute check-ins during the pilot produce actionable data that surveys do not

Create a feedback loop with visible consequences: when observer feedback leads to a product change, explicitly communicate 'We changed X because you told us Y' — this builds trust and increases engagement quality
Identify informal influencers: the team member who others ask for advice is a more important observer than the formal team lead. Cultivate this person as an adoption champion
Resistance to Claude integrations takes three common forms, each requiring a different response:
Senior leader actively opposing the programme in forums and decisions
Threat to budget, authority, or headcount; competing programme priority
Direct conversation with their specific concerns; champion-mediated dialogue; escalate to steering committee only if direct engagement fails
Legal or CISO repeatedly deferring approval without clear criteria
Insufficient information to make a risk decision; no precedent for AI in the organisation
Provide a security/privacy assessment template and complete it; request a meeting to walk through each concern; propose a limited pilot with enhanced monitoring to establish precedent
Users not engaging with the pilot; finding workarounds
Fear of job impact; lack of training; distrust of AI accuracy; process doesn't fit workflow
Structured change management programme; transparent communication about role impact; co-design session to fit tool to workflow; visible executive endorsement from a respected figure

For each resistant stakeholder, maintain a resistance tracker: the specific objection, the evidence provided to address it, the current status, and the next action. This keeps the engagement structured and creates an audit trail of what was communicated when — useful if resistance escalates to formal complaint.

# Stakeholder resistance tracking data model
# Used to maintain structured engagement records

stakeholder_resistance = {
    "stakeholder_id": "ciso-001",
    "name": "Alex Merchant",
    "role": "Chief Information Security Officer",
    "resistance_items": [
        {
            "concern": "Data sent to Anthropic API may include personal data subject to GDPR",
            "date_raised": "2025-03-05",
            "evidence_provided": [
                "Anthropic DPA with Standard Contractual Clauses (provided 2025-03-10)",
                "Data flow diagram showing PII redaction before API call (provided 2025-03-10)",
                "Transfer Impact Assessment completed and approved by DPO (2025-03-18)",
            ],
            "status": "resolved",
            "resolved_date": "2025-03-20",
            "notes": "CISO confirmed acceptance after reviewing TIA. Signed off on proceeding.",
        },
        {
            "concern": "No visibility on what data Anthropic stores from API calls",
            "date_raised": "2025-03-20",
            "evidence_provided": [
                "Anthropic data retention policy (0-day retention for API tier) shared 2025-03-22",
            ],
            "status": "pending_response",
            "next_action": "Follow up by 2025-03-28 on whether retention policy is acceptable",
            "notes": "CISO requested legal review of retention policy wording.",
        },
    ],
    "overall_sentiment": "neutral",
    "last_updated": "2025-03-22",
}
A stakeholder map without a communication plan is an analysis document. The communication plan translates the map into a scheduled, targeted engagement programme:
Solution architects, platform team, BU tech lead
The most common communication failures in enterprise Claude programmes:

Sending the same 20-page programme report to the CTO, the CISO, the users, and the compliance admin wastes everyone's time and signals that the team does not understand who they are communicating with.

Communications that only report positive progress without acknowledging risks or delays lose credibility when the inevitable difficulty arises. Building trust through honest communications early makes difficult news easier to deliver later.

Distributing documents and assuming they are read is not engagement. High-power stakeholders are busy; if they have not confirmed receipt and understood the key point, the communication did not happen effectively.
Every communication that requests a decision, an approval, or an acknowledgement needs a tracked follow-up. Items that fall into the gap between 'sent' and 'actioned' become programme risks.
Updating the Map Throughout the Programme

A stakeholder map created in discovery is accurate for discovery. By the time the programme reaches the pilot phase, stakeholder positions will have shifted — new stakeholders will have emerged, some will have moved between quadrants, and sentiment will have changed based on what they have seen and heard.
Schedule a formal stakeholder map review at the following programme milestones:
Technical complexity often surfaces new stakeholders in the platform and infrastructure teams who now need to be managed.
Pilot introduction is the first time most users encounter the system. Pre-pilot stakeholder assessment identifies who needs targeted change management before go-live.
Pilot feedback reveals resistance or enthusiasm that was not anticipated in the original map. Update sentiment scores based on observed behaviour, not stated intention.

Scaling requires new approvals — sometimes from new stakeholders (board-level sign-off for large deployments, regulator notification for highly regulated sectors). Re-map before the scale business case is presented.

The stakeholder map is not a deliverable — it is a living tool. A map that is completed in week one and never updated after that is a false confidence artifact. The programme team that uses the map actively, updating it based on observed behaviour and revisiting engagement strategies as conditions change, is the one that avoids surprise blocking events late in delivery.

Claude programmes that span multiple business units introduce stakeholder dynamics that single-BU programmes do not face. When two BUs share a Claude platform but have different success metrics, different sponsors, and different priorities, the stakeholder map must capture inter-BU relationships and potential conflicts, not just individual positions.

A programme with two BU sponsors — one in Operations and one in Finance — may produce situations where both sponsors have legitimate authority over programme decisions, but their interests diverge. Operations wants a tool that reduces processing time; Finance wants a tool that reduces headcount cost. These goals often overlap but sometimes conflict (a solution that processes faster but requires more reviewers satisfies Operations and not Finance).
Define distinct KPIs for each BU sponsor rather than a single shared KPI that neither fully owns. Each sponsor's section of the quarterly business review presents their own metric progress.

When both BU sponsors have decision authority, create a formal joint steering mechanism rather than allowing informal bilateral negotiations. A shared forum forces explicit priority decisions rather than allowing passive obstruction.
Define in the governance structure who breaks ties when sponsor priorities conflict. Without this, disagreements between sponsors stall delivery indefinitely.

Middle management — team leads, department heads, section managers — is systematically underweighted in stakeholder maps that focus on senior approval authorities and front-line users. Middle managers are often the decisive factor in adoption outcomes:
They control how their teams spend discretionary time — if they do not actively encourage Claude use, team members default to existing workflows
They translate executive mandates into operational reality — a positive CEO statement about AI means nothing if the team lead tells their team 'this is just another thing we have to do'
They surface the ground-level friction that prevents adoption — process gaps, system integration failures, edge cases the pilot did not cover
They carry the credibility with their teams that programme communications from corporate do not have

Engagement strategy for middle managers: identify 2–3 team leads per pilot BU and conduct individual 30-minute conversations early in discovery. These conversations serve dual purpose — gathering requirements from the people who know the work best, and creating early ownership of the outcome. A team lead who contributed to the use case design is more likely to support its deployment than one who received it as a fait accompli.

Stakeholder sentiment — how each key stakeholder currently feels about the Claude programme — is the dynamic data layer that sits on top of the static power-interest grid. Sentiment drives behaviour: a Gatekeeper with negative sentiment becomes an active blocker; a Champion with declining sentiment reduces their advocacy. Sentiment must be measured systematically, not inferred from sporadic impressions.
Current sentiment, new concerns, open questions
Labour-intensive; requires relationship to get candid responses
Anonymity limits follow-up; low response rate if not incentivised
Whether key stakeholders ask blocking questions or express concerns
Point-in-time; does not capture between-gate sentiment shifts
Proxy indicators (email response time, meeting attendance, escalation frequency)

Use a simple 5-point sentiment scale for key stakeholders: 1 = Actively opposed, 2 = Resistant, 3 = Neutral/Uncertain, 4 = Supportive, 5 = Actively championing. Track changes over time. A stakeholder who moves from 4 to 2 between reviews is a warning signal that requires immediate investigation and response.

# Simple stakeholder sentiment tracker
# Record sentiment scores at each engagement point

stakeholder_sentiments = [
    {
        "id": "dir-ops-001",
        "name": "Jo Fielding",
        "role": "Director of Operations",
        "quadrant": "champion",
        "sentiment_history": [
            {"date": "2025-01-15", "score": 4, "notes": "Positive at kickoff; concerned about timeline"},
            {"date": "2025-02-01", "score": 5, "notes": "After seeing early prototype; actively sharing with peers"},
            {"date": "2025-03-01", "score": 4, "notes": "Timeline slipped; still supportive but flagging visibility concern"},
        ],
        "current_score": 4,
        "trend": "stable",  # or "improving", "declining"
        "next_action": "Brief on revised timeline before March steering committee",
    },
    {
        "id": "ciso-001",
        "name": "Alex Merchant",
        "role": "CISO",
        "quadrant": "gatekeeper",
        "sentiment_history": [
            {"date": "2025-01-15", "score": 2, "notes": "Initial resistance: 'I need to understand what data goes to Anthropic'"},
            {"date": "2025-02-15", "score": 3, "notes": "After DPA and TIA review; neutral, no further objections raised"},
        ],
        "current_score": 3,
        "trend": "improving",
        "next_action": "Provide pre-gate 3 security summary; request conditional approval",
    },
]

def sentiment_label(score):
    labels = {1: "Actively opposed", 2: "Resistant", 3: "Neutral", 4: "Supportive", 5: "Championing"}
    return labels.get(score, "Unknown")

for s in stakeholder_sentiments:
    current = s["current_score"]
    print(f"{s['name']} ({s['role']}): {current}/5 - {sentiment_label(current)} [{s['trend']}]")
    print(f"  Next: {s['next_action']}")

# Jo Fielding (Director of Operations): 4/5 - Supportive [stable]
#   Next: Brief on revised timeline before March steering committee
# Alex Merchant (CISO): 3/5 - Neutral [improving]
#   Next: Provide pre-gate 3 security summary; request conditional approval

Healthcare, financial services, and public sector organisations have statutory stakeholder obligations that extend the standard mapping exercise. Failing to identify and manage regulatory stakeholders correctly can invalidate an entire deployment, regardless of technical quality.

In UK financial services, the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR) means that every material change to a regulated process has a named Senior Manager who is personally accountable for its oversight. If a Claude integration changes how a regulated activity (lending decisions, investment advice, complaints handling) is conducted, the responsible Senior Manager is a mandatory stakeholder with specific information obligations. Their sign-off is a regulatory requirement, not just a governance preference.

Additional financial services stakeholders to include: the Model Risk function (AI models in credit or trading processes require model risk governance review), the Chief Risk Officer (programme risk framework must be presented), and potentially the regulator (for novel use of AI in client-facing processes, a regulatory notification or pre-approval discussion may be required under Consumer Duty or FCA rules).

NHS and private healthcare Claude deployments interact with clinical governance structures that have no equivalent in other sectors. The Clinical Safety Officer (CSO) is a mandated role for software deployed in clinical settings — DCB 0129 (for manufacturers) and DCB 0160 (for NHS organisations deploying software) require hazard identification, risk assessment, and clinical safety case documentation. The CSO is a high-power, specialist-knowledge stakeholder who must be engaged in feasibility assessment, not just at deployment.

For any Claude integration that touches patient data or clinical decision support, add to the stakeholder map: the Caldicott Guardian (responsible for patient data governance), the Information Governance lead, the Clinical Governance committee, and the trust's Data Protection Officer.

Government departments face a combination of regulatory stakeholder obligations (the Information Commissioner's Office for DPIA requirements, the Central Digital and Data Office for AI standards compliance) and parliamentary accountability (ministers must be able to explain AI tools to parliamentary committees). The stakeholder map for a central government Claude deployment should include a communications lead who can brief ministerial private offices and prepare public-facing transparency statements.

In regulated industries, a stakeholder missed is not just a relationship management failure — it can be a compliance failure. Build the regulatory stakeholder layer into the mapping exercise from day one, before any technical work begins.
Influence Without Authority: The Delivery Manager Role

The programme delivery manager typically has no formal authority over the BU directors, CISOs, or technical leads they must influence. Influence without authority — the ability to move stakeholders without the power to compel them — is the core delivery management skill in enterprise AI programmes.

Stakeholders who are confused or uncertain about what is being asked of them will default to the status quo. The delivery manager who provides clear, specific, time-bound asks ('I need your approval on the data flow diagram by Thursday') gets decisions; the one who sends open-ended updates ('let me know your thoughts') does not.

Prepare a one-page decision summary for every gate review, with the options, the evidence for each, and a recommended action. A stakeholder who is given a well-prepared decision to sign off is more likely to act quickly than one who is expected to synthesise information and form a view independently.

Building genuine relationships by helping stakeholders with their own priorities — sharing relevant research, making useful introductions, flagging risks they care about before they become problems — creates goodwill that translates into cooperation when you need it.

'The Operations Director and the CFO have both approved this approach' reduces the psychological risk for the CISO who is on the fence. Strategic sequencing of stakeholder engagement — getting easy approvals first and citing them in later conversations — is legitimate influence.

Escalating a resistant stakeholder to their senior without exhausting direct engagement options damages the relationship and often produces compliance without buy-in. Reserve escalation for situations where direct engagement has genuinely failed and the blockage is causing programme harm.

Stakeholder mapping for a Claude programme requires identifying the full stakeholder set — including commonly overlooked approval chains, compliance functions, and downstream consumers — and placing each on the power-interest grid. Champions require co-creative engagement and active equipping; Gatekeepers require risk-framed evidence and pre-briefed approval paths; Observers require structured involvement in piloting and visible feedback loops; Monitors require minimal but non-zero engagement.

A stakeholder resistance tracker maintains structured records of each concern, evidence provided, and resolution status. A communication plan ensures that every segment receives appropriately formatted updates at the right cadence. The map is reviewed at each programme milestone and updated based on observed behaviour rather than assumed intent.
